message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To survey fertility patients’ agreement with ASRM recommendations during the COVID-19 pandemic and the emotional impact on them.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>An online survey was sent to current fertility patients</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>New York City academic fertility practice at the epicenter of the COVID-19 pandemic</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Patient(s)</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Fertility patients seen within the last year</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Intervention(s)</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>None</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome Measures(s)</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patient agreement with the ASRM recommendations during the COVID-19 pandemic and the emotional impact rated on a Likert scale.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Result(s)</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A total of 518 patients completed the survey for a response rate of 17%. Fifty percent of respondents had a cycle canceled due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Of those who had a cycle cancelled, 85% of respondents found it to be moderately to extremely upsetting with 22% rating it to be equivalent to the loss of a child. There was no difference on the emotional impact based on the type of cycle cancelled. Fifty-five percent of patients agreed that diagnostic procedures such as hysterosalpingograms should be cancelled while 36% of patients agreed all fertility cycles should be cancelled. Patients were slightly more likely to agree with the ASRM guidelines if they have an upcoming cycle cancelled (p = 0.041). Of all respondents 82% would have preferred to have the option to start a treatment cycle in consultation with their doctor.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion(s)</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Given the severity of the COVID-19 pandemic, the physical, financial and emotional impact of this unprecedented threat cannot be underestimated in our fertility patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: THE EMOTIONAL IMPACT OF THE ASRM GUIDELINES ON FERTILITY PATIENTS DURING THE COVID-19 PANDEMIC
